Based on the newly gathered evidence, particularly emphasizing points from references [7], [15], and [17]:
- Reference [7]: Clearly states that the lateral lingual swellings merge to form the anterior two-thirds of the tongue, confirming our earlier reasoning.
- Reference [15]: Explicitly mentions that the anterior two-thirds of the tongue derive from different embryological origins compared to the posterior one-third, reinforcing the distinction highlighted in Round 1.
- Reference [17]: Emphasizes that the anterior two-thirds of the tongue correspond to the developmental contributions from the first pharyngeal arch, consistent with our understanding.
Given this strong corroborative evidence from authoritative sources detailing the embryological basis and anatomical divisions of the tongue, I reaffirm my agreement with Option B ("Anterior 2/3 of tongue"). Therefore,

Tool Call: retrieve("anatomical origins of anterior portion of tongue")

Retrieved Documents: 32 documents

Based on the newly gathered evidence, particularly emphasizing points from references [7], [15], and [17]:
- Reference [7]: Clearly states that the lateral lingual swellings merge to form the anterior two-thirds of the tongue, confirming our earlier reasoning.
- Reference [15]: Explicitly mentions that the anterior two-thirds of the tongue derive from different embryological origins compared to the posterior one-third, reinforcing the distinction highlighted in Round 1.
- Reference [17]: Emphasizes that the anterior two-thirds of the tongue correspond to the developmental contributions from the first pharyngeal arch, consistent with our understanding.
Given this strong corroborative evidence from authoritative sources detailing the embryological basis and anatomical divisions of the tongue, I reaffirm my agreement with Option B ("Anterior 2/3 of tongue"). Therefore,
